Chapter 15: The Seat

The longhouse of Vossfjord smelled of old blood and fresh pine. Torches flickered along the central hearth, casting long shadows over the carved pillars that held up the roof. Chieftains filled the benches in their furs and silver, their voices a low rumble like the fjord before a storm. At the high seat, the great ring of the jarl gleamed in the firelight—too large for any hand but a king’s.

Eindride stood before it.

The torc burned against his brow, its weight a reminder of what he’d carried through snow and silence. His fingers flexed once, then stilled. Around him, the murmurs thickened.

Jorund didn’t sit. He stood by the door with his arms crossed, his scarred face a mask of barely controlled rage. Ragna sat at the end of the high bench, her shawl draped over her shoulders like a shroud. Her eyes were sharp, watching everything.

No one had spoken a word since Astrid left.

Then Hallvard the Red stood. His beard was streaked with frost, his knuckles brass-studded.

“Eindride Bjornsson,” he said, “you claim the seat of Vossfjord. You claim the chieftain’s ring. You claim the hand of Astrid Jorundsdottir to seal the bloodline.”

Eindride didn’t look at the ring. He looked at the empty space where Astrid should be.

“Aye,” he said.

Hallvard nodded once. “Then you must take the seat. And you must take the bride.”

Jorund spat. “She’s not his bride. She’s mine by oath and law.”

Ragna’s voice cut through the hall like a knife. “And what law is that, Jorund? The one you wrote after you carved his name from the ledgers? The one he never agreed to?”

Silence.

Jorund’s jaw tightened. “She spoke the words.”

“Words can be paid for,” Ragna said. “Oaths can be broken.”

A chieftain from the north rose. “Then let him sit. Let the Frostathing witness the claim.”

Another stood. “Let the torc be seen.”

Eindride stepped forward. He placed his palm flat on the ring. The silver was cold, unyielding.

“This seat was stolen,” he said. “The name was erased. The bride was taken. But the torc remains. The blood remains. And the law says a man may reclaim what was taken by force or by witness.”

Jorund lunged forward. “You think the law matters? The law is what I make it!”

He grabbed for the axe at his belt.

Eindride didn’t move. But from the shadows behind the high seat, two of Hallvard’s men stepped forward, blades drawn.

Ragna rose. “Jorund Hjaldarson,” she said, “you stand accused before your peers. You stole the name of another. You took his bride. You burned his ledgers. You broke every oath you swore.”

Jorund’s breath came fast. “She was never his bride. She was promised to me in blood!”

Ragna’s eyes were dark, unflinching. “She was promised to him in blood first. And the blood still flows.”

The hall held its breath.

Then Hallvard raised a hand. “Enough. The Frostathing has heard the claim. The torc is witnessed. The seat is empty. Let the rightful heir take it.”

Eindride reached into his cloak.

The torc came free.

Silver glinted in the torchlight, the Ynglingar raven etched deep into its curve. The chieftains leaned forward, murmurs rippling through the hall like wind through the pines.

Jorund’s face went white.

Ragna didn’t blink.

Eindride lifted the torc high.

Then he placed it upon his brow.

The hall erupted.

Shouts. Gasps. Hands reaching for weapons, then falling back as understanding settled like winter frost. The torc was real. The heir was real. The rightful jarl had returned.

Jorund wrenched free of his guards and stormed toward the door.

“This is not over,” he snarled.

“It was over the moment you took the name,” Eindride said, his voice low and steady.

Jorund turned back, his face twisted. “You’ll regret this. I’ll take everything from you—your life, your claim, your precious bride.”

Eindride met his gaze. “Try.”

Jorund stormed out, the door slamming behind him.

The hall fell silent again.

Ragna stepped forward. She walked slowly, her skirts whispering against the benches. She stopped before Eindride, her eyes flicking from the torc to his face.

“You wear your mother’s mark well,” she said.

“As did she,” Eindride replied.

Ragna exhaled. “She would have been proud.”

“She was.”

A pause. The torches flickered.

Then Astrid stepped into the hall from the women’s doorway.

She wore a simple grey dress, her hair braided tight against her scalp. No jewelry. No adornment. Only the quiet truth of her presence.

She walked forward.

Eindride felt the weight of every eye in the hall. He didn’t look away from her.

Astrid stopped before him.

She knelt.

The hall gasped.

Eindride’s breath caught.

Astrid’s head was bent, her forehead nearly touching the edge of the torc that rested on his brow.

She whispered.

“Jorund did not act alone. The scroll names the traitors. Your brother’s allies. His oath-breakers. His false witnesses.”

She lifted her head.

Her eyes met his.

“They will burn for their lies.”

The torc gleamed.

And in that moment, the raven’s cry echoed over the fjord—soft, distant, almost like a warning.
